finding.reachability: Any remote attacker who can send HTTP requests can forge all non-connection-level IP headers (x-client-ip, x-forwarded-for, cf-connecting-ip, fastly-client-ip, true-client-ip, x-real-ip, x-cluster-client-ip, x-forwarded, forwarded-for, forwarded, x-appengine-user-ip, Cf-Pseudo-IPv4); the library reads them verbatim before falling through to connection.remoteAddress.
finding.justification: Client-supplied headers are prioritised over TCP-level remoteAddress without any trusted-proxy allowlist, so any caller using this IP for access control can be bypassed by setting a spoofed header.

detection_pattern.description: Libraries that return a client IP from user-controlled headers without first verifying the upstream proxy is trusted enable IP-spoofing attacks that bypass IP-based authentication and rate-limiting.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed. getClientIp (lines 39-121 of lib/index.js) reads attacker-controlled HTTP headers (x-client-ip, x-forwarded-for, cf-connecting-ip, fastly-client-ip, true-client-ip, x-real-ip, x-cluster-client-ip, x-forwarded, forwarded-for, forwarded, x-appengine-user-ip, Cf-Pseudo-IPv4) in strict priority order before falling through to TCP-level sources (req.connection.remoteAddress, req.socket.remoteAddress). The only validation performed is a regex-based syntactic IP format check via is.ip() in lib/is.js — this confirms the header value is a valid-looking IP address but does nothing to verify the upstream proxy is trusted. There is no trusted-proxy allowlist anywhere in the codebase: the mw() function accepts only an attributeName option (line 130), and there is no isTrustedProxy, proxyList, or TRUSTED_PROXIES mechanism. The vulnerable path is the default code path — no non-default options or configuration are required to trigger it. No schema validation (zod/joi/ajv) is present. Any remote attacker who can send an HTTP request can set X-Client-IP: <spoofed-ip> (or any of the other checked headers) with a syntactically valid IP address and the library will return that spoofed value as the authoritative client IP. The README documents the library's use case as geolocation but does not warn against access-control use; the library is widely used in security-relevant contexts in practice. Severity is medium: the vulnerability is a real design gap (no trusted-proxy enforcement), but exploitation requires the caller to use the returned IP for access control, and many deployments sit behind a trusted reverse proxy that strips/overwrites these headers before they reach the app.

reachability.reasoning: Both getClientIp and mw are explicitly exported via module.exports and are the primary public API. The mw() middleware requires no options (options defaults to {}) and its only accepted option (attributeName) controls only the property name attached to req, not header trust behavior. The vulnerable code path — reading attacker-controlled headers (x-client-ip, x-forwarded-for, cf-connecting-ip, fastly-client-ip, true-client-ip, x-real-ip, x-cluster-client-ip, x-forwarded, forwarded-for, forwarded, x-appengine-user-ip, Cf-Pseudo-IPv4) in strict priority order before TCP-level sources — executes on every single request with zero configuration. The only check performed is is.ip() which validates that the header value is syntactically a valid IP address; it does not verify the upstream sender is a trusted proxy. There is no trusted-proxy allowlist, no isTrustedProxy mechanism, no proxyList, and no TRUSTED_PROXIES concept anywhere in the codebase. Any remote attacker who can send an HTTP request can set X-Client-IP (or any of the other checked headers) to a syntactically valid spoofed IP and the library will return that value as the authoritative client IP. No non-default options are required to trigger the vulnerability. Severity is kept at medium (not raised) because exploitation requires the caller to use the returned IP for access control or rate-limiting, and deployments behind a properly-configured reverse proxy that strips/overwrites these headers before they reach the application are not affected.

finding.reachability: Any remote attacker who can send HTTP requests can forge all non-connection-level IP headers (x-client-ip, x-forwarded-for, cf-connecting-ip, fastly-client-ip, true-client-ip, x-real-ip, x-cluster-client-ip, x-forwarded, forwarded-for, forwarded, x-appengine-user-ip, Cf-Pseudo-IPv4); the library reads them verbatim before falling through to connection.remoteAddress.
finding.justification: Client-supplied headers are prioritised over TCP-level remoteAddress without any trusted-proxy allowlist, so any caller using this IP for access control can be bypassed by setting a spoofed header.

detection_pattern.description: Libraries that return a client IP from user-controlled headers without first verifying the upstream proxy is trusted enable IP-spoofing attacks that bypass IP-based authentication and rate-limiting.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed. getClientIp (lines 39-121 of lib/index.js) reads attacker-controlled HTTP headers (x-client-ip, x-forwarded-for, cf-connecting-ip, fastly-client-ip, true-client-ip, x-real-ip, x-cluster-client-ip, x-forwarded, forwarded-for, forwarded, x-appengine-user-ip, Cf-Pseudo-IPv4) in strict priority order before falling through to TCP-level sources (req.connection.remoteAddress, req.socket.remoteAddress). The only validation performed is a regex-based syntactic IP format check via is.ip() in lib/is.js — this confirms the header value is a valid-looking IP address but does nothing to verify the upstream proxy is trusted. There is no trusted-proxy allowlist anywhere in the codebase: the mw() function accepts only an attributeName option (line 130), and there is no isTrustedProxy, proxyList, or TRUSTED_PROXIES mechanism. The vulnerable path is the default code path — no non-default options or configuration are required to trigger it. No schema validation (zod/joi/ajv) is present. Any remote attacker who can send an HTTP request can set X-Client-IP: <spoofed-ip> (or any of the other checked headers) with a syntactically valid IP address and the library will return that spoofed value as the authoritative client IP. The README documents the library's use case as geolocation but does not warn against access-control use; the library is widely used in security-relevant contexts in practice. Severity is medium: the vulnerability is a real design gap (no trusted-proxy enforcement), but exploitation requires the caller to use the returned IP for access control, and many deployments sit behind a trusted reverse proxy that strips/overwrites these headers before they reach the app.

finding.location: is.ip() in lib/is.js line 5, called from getClientIp on every header value
finding.reachability: Any remote attacker who can send a single HTTP request; every header value (x-client-ip, x-forwarded-for entries, etc.) is passed to is.ip() which applies the complex IPv6 regex.
finding.justification: The IPv6 regex contains nested repeating groups (([:A-F]{1,4}:(:| )|){5}) whose alternation creates exponential backtracking paths; a crafted string such as 'A:A:A:A:A:A:A:A:A:!A' can pin the event-loop thread for seconds, constituting a denial-of-service.

detection_pattern.section_name: ReDoS: complex IPv6 regex on user input
detection_pattern.description: Hand-rolled IPv6 regexes with nested quantifiers applied to HTTP header values are a well-known ReDoS vector; Node.js's built-in net.isIP() provides a safe, O(n) alternative.

finding.reachability: Any caller that can pass a synthetic or mutated req object where req.raw references back to req (circular) — realistic in test harnesses, proxy middleware chains, or Fastify where req.raw is normally distinct but could be overridden.
finding.justification: The function recurses unconditionally into getClientIp(req.raw) with no depth guard or cycle detection; a circular reference (req.raw = req) causes unlimited recursion and a stack overflow that crashes the Node process.

detection_pattern.description: Request-unwrapping helpers that recurse on req.raw without a depth limit or cycle guard are vulnerable to stack overflow if the wrapped object is ever self-referential.

verdict.reasoning: The recursive call at lines 116-118 (if (is.existy(req.raw)) { return getClientIp(req.raw); }) is confirmed present with no depth guard or cycle detection. is.existy only checks value != null, so a circular reference req.raw === req would cause unlimited recursion and a stack overflow. However, the reachability claim is critically flawed. The req parameter is the HTTP request object constructed by the framework (Express, Fastify, etc.). HTTP attackers control headers, body, and URL — not JavaScript object properties like .raw. No HTTP input path sets req.raw to point back to req. In Fastify, req.raw is the underlying Node IncomingMessage (req.raw !== req under normal operation). To trigger this, an attacker must be able to assign a JavaScript property on the req object inside the same process, which requires either: (1) a developer bug creating an accidental circular reference, or (2) malicious code running in the same Node process. There is no schema validation (zod/joi/ajv) involved because the input is an object reference, not user-supplied data. The vulnerability is not reachable via external HTTP requests. The finding conflates a theoretical code quality concern (missing cycle guard) with an exploitable network-level DoS. The code pattern exists but the described attack vector does not apply to real deployments under a normal HTTP threat model.

finding.reachability: Requires an attacker-controlled options object passed to mw(); typical in multi-tenant setups where configuration originates from user data or config files.
finding.justification: configuration.attributeName is used directly as the property name in Object.defineProperty(req, attributeName, ...) without validation; passing '__proto__', 'constructor', or any existing req property name can shadow built-ins or overwrite framework internals on the request object.

detection_pattern.description: Passing an attacker-controlled string as the property name argument to Object.defineProperty can shadow prototype properties or overwrite security-relevant fields on the target object.

verdict.reasoning:
The code at mw() lines 130-138 does use `configuration.attributeName` directly as the property name in `Object.defineProperty(req, attributeName, ...)` with no validation. The sink exists. However, the finding is not real as described for several compounding reasons:

1. REACHABILITY IS WRONG: `mw(options)` is called ONCE at application startup, not per HTTP request. The returned middleware closure captures `attributeName` via closure at initialization time. An attacker must control the application's startup configuration (options object), not an HTTP request parameter. This is configuration-injection, not request-injection. The 'multi-tenant' scenario described is speculative and not supported by the API design.

2. __PROTO__ DOES NOT CAUSE PROTOTYPE POLLUTION: The most severe claim — that passing '__proto__' as attributeName can pollute Object.prototype — is empirically false. `Object.defineProperty(obj, '__proto__', descriptor)` creates an own enumerable property named '__proto__' on `obj` itself. It does NOT modify `Object.prototype`. Verified with Node.js: `Object.prototype` remains unaffected, and new objects do not inherit the getter.

3. ACTUAL IMPACT IS PER-OBJECT ONLY: If an attacker somehow controls `attributeName` at startup, they could shadow properties on each individual `req` object (e.g., setting `req.method` to always return the IP string). This is confined to individual req objects and does not propagate globally. Impact is limited to potential DoS of request routing if a critical req property is overwritten.

4. NO DOCUMENTED USER-CONTROLLED PATH: The README and API show `mw()` is called with developer-hardcoded options (e.g., `app.use(requestIp.mw({ attributeName: 'ip' }))`). There is no documented or common pattern where HTTP request data feeds into the mw() options object.

5. NO SCHEMA VALIDATION BUT IRRELEVANT: There is no zod/joi/ajv validation of `attributeName`, confirming the absence of a guard — but the guard is irrelevant because the attack prerequisite (runtime control of startup config) is essentially equivalent to arbitrary code execution already.

finding.reachability: Any request where a header is present but set to null (some HTTP frameworks allow this); the bug is structural and affects every is.ip() call in getClientIp.
finding.justification: Operator precedence makes the expression evaluate as (existy(value) && ipv4.test(value)) || ipv6.test(value); the existy guard is not applied to the IPv6 branch, so ipv6.test(null) — which coerces null to the string 'null' — runs unconditionally, bypassing the intended null guard and feeding the complex regex unexpected input.

detection_pattern.section_name: Logic bug: missing null guard before second regex branch
detection_pattern.description: When a null-guard is combined with two regex alternatives using && and || without parentheses, operator precedence bypasses the guard for the second alternative, running a potentially complex regex on null/undefined input.

verdict.reasoning: The operator precedence bug in is.js line 19 is structurally real but has no exploitable security impact. The code reads: `return existy(value) && regexes.ipv4.test(value) || regexes.ipv6.test(value);` which evaluates as `(existy(value) && ipv4.test(value)) || ipv6.test(value)` due to && binding tighter than ||. This means ipv6.test() runs unconditionally even when value is null or undefined. However, the claimed impact does not materialize: (1) JavaScript's RegExp.prototype.test() coerces null to the string 'null' and undefined to 'undefined'; (2) neither 'null' nor 'undefined' matches the IPv6 regex — confirmed by direct execution: ipv6.test(null) === false and ipv6.test(undefined) === false; (3) the only values where existy() returns false are null and undefined (via != null check); (4) for those exact values, ipv6.test() also returns false, so is.ip(null) and is.ip(undefined) correctly return false under the buggy code, identical to the intended behavior. The finding claims the guard is 'bypassed' allowing unexpected input to reach the regex - this is technically true but produces no different outcome. No auth bypass, no injection, no false positive IP acceptance is possible. The bug is a code quality/defensive coding issue at most, not a security vulnerability. The 'low' severity overstates the actual risk which is effectively zero.

finding.reachability: Any caller who passes attacker-controlled input to is.ip() or is.not.ip() — in the request-ip package this is HTTP request header values (e.g., X-Forwarded-For), so it is network-reachable without authentication.
finding.justification: The IPv6 regex contains the sub-expression `([\dA-F]{1,4}:(:| )|){5}` where the empty alternative `|` allows the quantifier to match 5 zero-width tokens, creating up to 2^5 ambiguous parse paths; combined with the backreference-dependent lookaheads `(?!\3)` and `(?!\2\3)` in the second half, this is a well-known ReDoS-class regex that was exploitable on Node <18 (naive NFA). V8 in Node 22.x JIT-compiles it to linear time, so exploitation requires an older runtime.

detection_pattern.section_name: ReDoS: IPv6 static regex with ambiguous quantified alternatives
detection_pattern.description: Catches static IPv6-validation regexes that repeat a group with an empty alternative `|)` 3 or more times — the classic pattern that causes polynomial backtracking in non-JIT NFA engines (Node <18, older browsers).

verdict.reasoning:
The regex at regexes.ipv6 (line 5 of is.js) is structurally flagged as unsafe by safe-regex (returns false), and does contain backreferences (\2, \3 in lookaheads) plus a quantified group with an empty alternative `([\dA-F]{1,4}:(:|\b)|){5}` that can match zero-width tokens. The reachability is confirmed: HTTP header values (X-Forwarded-For, x-client-ip, cf-connecting-ip, fastly-client-ip, true-client-ip, and others) flow directly from req.headers into is.ip() -> regexes.ipv6.test() with no sanitization, schema validation (no zod/joi/ajv), no auth guard, and no length cap within the package itself.

However, empirical testing on Node 22.22.2 (the runtime present) shows completely flat/constant-time behavior regardless of input length: 1000-group malicious inputs with crafted patterns designed to trigger worst-case backtracking complete in 0.018-0.176ms. The timing does not grow exponentially or polynomially with input length. The finding itself acknowledges this: 'V8 in Node 22.x JIT-compiles it to linear time, so exploitation requires an older runtime.'

The vulnerability is conditionally real only on Node <18, where V8's backtracking NFA engine would process this regex without linear-time guarantees. On Node 18+ (and especially 22.x, the version present), the engine handles this in linear/constant time and the regex cannot be used to trigger ReDoS. Since no Node <18 runtime is in scope and the finding itself concedes modern Node immunity, this does not meet the bar for a confirmed, exploitable vulnerability in the current deployment context. Severity is downgraded to informational.

finding.reachability: Any caller passing null/undefined or a non-string value to is.ip(). In request-ip the value comes from request header parsing which may produce missing headers.
finding.justification: The expression `existy(value) && regexes.ipv4.test(value) || regexes.ipv6.test(value)` evaluates as `(existy(value) && ipv4.test(value)) || ipv6.test(value)` due to JS operator precedence — the null-guard `existy()` only protects the IPv4 branch; the IPv6 branch always executes, causing `RegExp.prototype.test(null)` to silently coerce null to the string 'null' and test it. The current IPv6 regex does not match 'null', so no exploitable false positive exists today, but it is a correctness defect that could surface under regex changes.

detection_pattern.section_name: Logic: null guard bypassed by operator precedence in OR chain
detection_pattern.description: Detects patterns where a null/existy guard is &&-chained with one test but a second test is ||-chained at the same level, meaning the guard does not protect the second test and null/undefined gets silently coerced to a string.

verdict.reasoning: The operator precedence defect on line 19 of is.js is confirmed real as a code quality issue: `existy(value) && regexes.ipv4.test(value) || regexes.ipv6.test(value)` evaluates as `(existy(value) && ipv4.test(value)) || ipv6.test(value)` due to JS && binding tighter than ||. This means `regexes.ipv6.test(null)` is called when value is null/undefined, coercing null to the string 'null' via RegExp.prototype.test. However, runtime testing confirms the current IPv6 regex does NOT match the coerced strings 'null' or 'undefined' — both return false. The observable behavior of is.ip(null), is.ip(undefined), and is.not.ip(null) is identical between the buggy expression and a correctly parenthesized expression. There is no false positive (null does not pass as a valid IP), no false negative (null correctly returns false), and no attacker-controllable path to exploit the precedence difference. Node.js HTTP headers return undefined (not null) for missing headers, and the getClientIpFromXForwardedFor caller has its own is.existy() guard and type check before reaching is.ip(). The finding's own justification concedes 'no exploitable false positive exists today' and labels this a 'correctness defect.' Under the default-false standard, this does not constitute a security vulnerability.
